note_data.title: Bank reconciliation statements
keyPoints: **Bank reconciliation statement** is prepared to explain the differences between the cash book balance and the bank statement balance
**Unpresented cheques** are cheques issued by the business but not yet presented to the bank for payment
**Outstanding deposits** are amounts paid into the bank but not yet recorded on the bank statement
**Bank charges** and **interest received** appear on the bank statement before being recorded in the cash book
**Dishonoured cheques** are cheques that have been returned by the bank due to insufficient funds
Start the reconciliation with either the **cash book balance** or **bank statement balance** and adjust for timing differences
**Direct debits** and **standing orders** may appear on the bank statement but not in the cash book
**Bank errors** must be corrected by contacting the bank, while **cash book errors** require journal entries
The **adjusted cash book balance** must equal the **adjusted bank statement balance** after reconciliation

definitions.definition: A statement prepared to explain the differences between the balance shown in the cash book and the balance shown on the bank statement
Cheques that have been issued and recorded in the cash book but have not yet been presented to the bank for payment
Amounts that have been paid into the bank and recorded in the cash book but do not yet appear on the bank statement
A cheque that has been returned by the bank unpaid, usually due to insufficient funds in the drawer's account

howToAnswer.instruction: Create a full bank reconciliation statement showing all adjustments clearly with proper headings and calculations
Give the reason why the difference exists, e.g. 'This is because the cheque was issued but not yet presented to the bank'
Show the adjusted balance after adding/subtracting the reconciling items

modelAnswer.question: The cash book shows a bank balance of Rs.15,400 (debit). The bank statement shows a balance of Rs.12,800 (credit). Unpresented cheques total Rs.3,200 and outstanding deposits are Rs.600. Prepare a bank reconciliation statement.
modelAnswer.answer:
Bank Reconciliation Statement as at [date]

Balance as per bank statement: Rs.12,800
Add: Outstanding deposits: Rs.600
: Rs.13,400
Less: Unpresented cheques: Rs.3,200
Adjusted bank balance: Rs.10,200

Balance as per cash book: Rs.15,400
Adjusted cash book balance: Rs.15,400

Note: There appears to be an error of Rs.5,200 that needs investigation.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Starting with bank statement balance Rs.12,800
Mark 5: Showing adjusted bank balance Rs.10,200
Mark 6: Comparing with cash book balance

commonMistakes: Never add unpresented cheques to bank statement balance — always subtract them because they reduce the bank balance
Students often confuse outstanding deposits with outstanding cheques — deposits increase bank balance, cheques decrease it
Never start reconciliation without stating which balance you are starting from (cash book or bank statement)
Students forget to update cash book for items appearing only on bank statement like bank charges or interest
Common error: treating credit balance as debit balance — always check if account is overdrawn
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 exam, usually worth 8-12 marks
Always show clear headings: 'Balance as per bank statement' or 'Balance as per cash book'
Remember the rule: Add outstanding deposits, subtract unpresented cheques when starting from bank statement
If balances don't reconcile after adjustments, state 'difference requires investigation'
Pakistani banks commonly use direct debits for utility bills — these often appear as reconciling items
formulas: Adjusted bank balance = Bank statement balance + Outstanding deposits - Unpresented cheques
Reconciliation check: Adjusted cash book balance = Adjusted bank statement balance
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Bank reconciliation statement format showing bank statement balance at top, adding outstanding deposits, subtracting unpresented cheques, and comparing final adjusted balance with cash book balance
